label.summary: Your FTP server is still using the password it shipped with, which means anyone who finds it can log straight in without knowing anything about you or your network. Everything else checked in this scan came back clean.

findings.title: Your FTP server still has its factory password
findings.what_it_means: The FTP file-sharing service running on your network never had its original username and password (admin/admin) changed. That's the same login every device of this type ships with out of the box.
findings.why_it_matters: Because this password is public knowledge, anyone who stumbles across this device — including automated tools that scan the internet for exactly this — can log in immediately and get at whatever files it holds.
findings.how_to_fix:
1. Log into ftp and change the default username/password immediately — use a strong password you don't use anywhere else.
2. Make sure ftp's login page isn't reachable from the open internet — restrict it to your home network only.
3. If you don't recognize or use ftp, consider taking it offline.

good_news: The file-sharing service on port 445 didn't turn up any known problems during this scan.

label.summary: Nothing concerning was found on your network this time — both items checked came back clean.
good_news: Your outgoing mail service (port 25) showed no known issues.
Your router's web interface responded normally, and no vulnerabilities were flagged for it.

label.summary: The one item checked in this scan — your router's web login — came back clean.
good_news: Your router's admin web page was checked and no known security problems were found.

label.summary: A web application server on your network is still logging in with its out-of-the-box password, which is an easy target — this needs fixing now. The other service checked out fine.

findings.title: Web application server logging in with a default password
findings.what_it_means: A web server (Apache Tomcat) on port 8080 is still set up with its original default login (admin/1234) rather than a password you chose yourself.
findings.why_it_matters: Default logins like this one are published in setup guides and known to attackers, so anyone who finds this server can walk right in and take it over without needing to guess anything.
findings.how_to_fix:
1. Log into http-proxy and change the default username/password immediately — use a strong password you don't use anywhere else.
2. Make sure http-proxy's login page isn't reachable from the open internet — restrict it to your home network only.
3. If you don't recognize or use http-proxy, consider taking it offline.

good_news: The Docker service on port 2375 didn't show any known vulnerabilities in this scan.

label.summary: Your VPN service is still using the manufacturer's default login instead of a password of your own, so it needs attention right away. The database service nearby checked out fine.

findings.title: VPN service still uses its factory login
findings.what_it_means: The OpenVPN service on port 1194 is still reachable using the default account (root/root) it came with, rather than credentials you set yourself.
findings.why_it_matters: A VPN is meant to be a secure gateway into your network — if its login is still the factory default, anyone who finds it can use that same door to get into everything behind it.
findings.how_to_fix:
1. Log into openvpn and change the default username/password immediately — use a strong password you don't use anywhere else.
2. Make sure openvpn's login page isn't reachable from the open internet — restrict it to your home network only.
3. If you don't recognize or use openvpn, consider taking it offline.

good_news: Your MySQL database on port 3306 showed nothing concerning in this scan.

label.summary: Your router's web login is still set to the password it came with in the box — that's a quick way in for anyone who finds it, so it should be changed right away. The other service checked came back clean.

findings.title: Router web login still set to factory default
findings.what_it_means: The web management page for your router (port 80) is still using the built-in default login (admin/admin) instead of a password you picked.
findings.why_it_matters: Router admin pages control your whole home network, and a default password on one is one of the very first things an attacker — or an automated scanning tool — will try.
findings.how_to_fix:
1. Log into http and change the default username/password immediately — use a strong password you don't use anywhere else.
2. Make sure http's login page isn't reachable from the open internet — restrict it to your home network only.
3. If you don't recognize or use http, consider taking it offline.

good_news: The Docker service on port 2375 didn't raise any known concerns.

label.summary: A database on your network can be accessed using its original factory login, which is worth fixing immediately since it's an easy target. The other service found nearby is fine.

findings.title: Database still using its out-of-the-box login
findings.what_it_means: Your MariaDB database (port 3306) can still be logged into with the default account (admin/1234) it shipped with, rather than a password unique to you.
findings.why_it_matters: A database often holds the most sensitive information on a network, and a well-known default login means anyone who reaches it can read or change that data without any real effort.
findings.how_to_fix:
1. Log into mysql and change the default username/password immediately — use a strong password you don't use anywhere else.
2. Make sure mysql's login page isn't reachable from the open internet — restrict it to your home network only.
3. If you don't recognize or use mysql, consider taking it offline.

good_news: Your FTP service on port 21 didn't show any known issues in this scan.

label.summary: A container management service on your network is wide open because it's still using its default login — this should be fixed right away. The FTP service nearby is fine.

findings.title: Docker management service left on its default login
findings.what_it_means: The Docker service on port 2375 is still reachable using its built-in default account (root/root) instead of a login you set up.
findings.why_it_matters: Docker's management interface can be used to start or control programs on this machine, so a default login here can hand an attacker much more than just this one service — potentially the whole device.
findings.how_to_fix:
1. Log into docker and change the default username/password immediately — use a strong password you don't use anywhere else.
2. Make sure docker's login page isn't reachable from the open internet — restrict it to your home network only.
3. If you don't recognize or use docker, consider taking it offline.

findings.title: Web application server left on its default login
findings.what_it_means: A Jetty web application server on port 8080 is still accepting its built-in default login (root/root) rather than a password unique to your setup.
findings.why_it_matters: A default login on a web application server is one of the first things attackers try, since it requires no guessing at all — it hands over full access immediately.
findings.how_to_fix:
1. Log into http-proxy and change the default username/password immediately — use a strong password you don't use anywhere else.
2. Make sure http-proxy's login page isn't reachable from the open internet — restrict it to your home network only.
3. If you don't recognize or use http-proxy, consider taking it offline.
